title: The destination, and the grades ladder
paras: Let's start with the destination, and the grades ladder that gets you there. "Chartered Town Planner" means Chartered Membership of the R-T-P-I — the grade that lets you use the letters M-R-T-P-I. The R-T-P-I is the body that charters planners in the U-K, and chartered status is the recognised mark of a qualified professional. Many senior and consultancy roles expect it, or require it. You reach it by passing the R-T-P-I's Assessment of Professional Competence — the A-P-C — and the various "routes" are really just different ways of getting to that assessment, depending on your qualifications and experience.

It helps to see the membership grades as a ladder. You're a Student member while you're studying on an accredited course — usually free. Then Licentiate, once you have an accredited qualification and are working towards the A-P-C. Then Associate — Assoc-R-T-P-I — a professional grade for those contributing to planning without a fully accredited qualification, and a stepping stone towards chartered status. Then Chartered — M-R-T-P-I — the goal: full professional membership, reached by passing the A-P-C. And finally Fellow, F-R-T-P-I — a senior grade for experienced chartered members, awarded on nomination. Not something you apply for early on.

title: The two things every route needs
paras: Now, the two things every route needs. Whatever your starting point, chartered status comes down to two ingredients: an accredited academic grounding in planning, and demonstrated professional competence through experience, tested by the A-P-C. The routes differ mainly in how you satisfy the first.

Take the academic side first. The R-T-P-I accredits planning qualifications at universities, and accreditation covers two things. There's a Spatial Planning component — the broad planning knowledge every planner needs — and a Specialist component, deeper expertise in a chosen area, such as urban design, environmental planning or economic development. A fully accredited qualification, often a "combined" accredited Masters, covers both. Some undergraduate degrees cover only the Spatial Planning component, in which case you "top up" with an accredited Specialist Masters. The practical rule is this: check that any course you're considering is R-T-P-I accredited, and whether it's accredited as combined — covering both components — or partial, because that determines whether you need anything further. The R-T-P-I publishes the list of accredited courses.

Then the experience side. Academic knowledge alone doesn't make you chartered. You must show, through the A-P-C, that you can apply planning skills competently in practice — evidenced by real professional experience, gained under the guidance of experienced colleagues. That's what the rest of this guide, and the A-P-C section, is about.

title: Routes at a glance
paras: Here are the routes at a glance. Find your starting point, then read the section that follows in full. The times I give are indicative — they depend on your qualifications, your hours and your experience — so confirm the current requirements with the R-T-P-I.

If you have an accredited planning Masters or a combined degree, your route is the Licentiate A-P-C — the L-A-P-C. You join as a Licentiate, gain two years' experience — one of them as a Licentiate — and pass the L-A-P-C. Around two to three years.

If you have an accredited undergraduate degree covering Spatial Planning only, you top up. Add an accredited Specialist Masters, then take the L-A-P-C. That's an extra one to two years' study, then the A-P-C.
If your degree is in another subject, it's an accredited conversion Masters plus the L-A-P-C — or the apprenticeship. One to two years' study, then two to three.

If you're working in planning with no accredited degree, it's the Associate A-P-C — or study, or the apprenticeship. You use your experience to become an Associate, then progress to Chartered. Around four to five years.

If you're employed and want to earn while you learn, there's the Degree Apprenticeship at Level 7. Your employer sponsors you, you combine work with university, and you gain the accredited Masters and sit an end-point A-P-C. Three to four years.

And if you're a surveyor or another built-environment professional, it's the Experienced Practitioner A-P-C — or a Masters plus the L-A-P-C. You demonstrate the competencies through substantial planning experience, so the timing varies with that experience.

title: If you have an accredited planning degree
paras: First, then — if you have an accredited planning degree. This is the most direct route, and it's the Licentiate A-P-C, the L-A-P-C. If you hold a fully accredited planning qualification — typically an accredited Masters, or an accredited undergraduate degree that covers both the Spatial Planning and Specialist components — you can join the R-T-P-I as a Licentiate and start working towards the A-P-C straight away.

You need a minimum of two years' professional planning experience — that's full-time equivalent, and part-time counts pro-rata — of which at least one year must be gained while you're registered as a Licentiate. Then you submit the L-A-P-C. Most accredited graduates reach chartered status two to three years after finishing their course, alongside a normal planning job. And do start keeping your reflective journal from your first day in practice — it's far easier than reconstructing two years of work from memory later.

What if your undergraduate degree is only partly accredited? Some accredited undergraduate degrees cover only the Spatial Planning component. If that's you, you're part of the way there. You "top up" by completing an accredited Specialist Masters — often one year full-time, or two part-time — which then gives you the full accredited grounding and access to the L-A-P-C. Check exactly what your undergraduate degree was accredited for before assuming you need, or don't need, the top-up.

title: If your degree is in another subject
paras: What if your degree is in another subject? Well, a great many planners did their first degree in something else — geography, law, architecture, environmental science, economics, history — and converted. Planning is an open profession, and there's a well-trodden path in.

The cleanest route is an accredited conversion Masters. Many R-T-P-I accredited Masters are designed for graduates of any discipline, and assume no prior planning study. They're typically one year full-time or two years part-time, and several universities offer part-time and distance-learning versions, so you can study while working. Complete an accredited combined Masters and you're in exactly the same position as any other accredited graduate: you join as a Licentiate and take the L-A-P-C. This is the surest way to full chartered status, and the one most career-changers take.

There are alternatives, if study first doesn't suit you. If you're already employed in a planning role, the degree apprenticeship lets you gain the accredited Masters and chartered status while working, and without paying tuition fees. And if you're already working in planning and building experience, the Associate and Experienced Practitioner routes may fit. But for most non-planning graduates who are free to study, an accredited Masters is the direct answer.

title: If you work in planning without an accredited degree
paras: Next — if you work in planning without an accredited degree. Plenty of capable people work in planning — as support officers, technicians, enforcement or validation staff, planning administrators — without an accredited planning qualification, and want to become chartered. The R-T-P-I has routes built for exactly this, and you have three realistic options.

The first is the Associate route. The Associate A-P-C is designed for people contributing to planning who don't hold a fully accredited qualification. Depending on your educational background, you'll need a band of relevant planning experience — broadly in the region of two to seven years — to apply, and success gives you the grade of Associate, Assoc-R-T-P-I. Associate is a recognised professional grade in its own right, and it's also a stepping stone: there's a defined route from Associate to full Chartered membership. Realistically, allow something like four to five years from starting out to chartered status by this path.

The second option is to study for an accredited qualification. You can study part-time, or by distance learning, while keeping your job — then take the L-A-P-C like any accredited graduate. It's more effort up front, but it gives you the full accredited grounding.

And the third is the degree apprenticeship. If your employer will support it, the Level 7 degree apprenticeship is often the best of all worlds for someone already in a planning job. You earn while you learn, you gain an accredited Masters and chartered status together, and the training is funded rather than paid for out of your own pocket. For a planning administrator with a supportive employer, this is frequently the smart move.

title: If you are a surveyor or other built-environment professional
paras: Now, if you're a surveyor or other built-environment professional. Chartered surveyors — M-R-I-C-S — architects, engineers, lawyers and others often move into planning, and dual qualification — for example, M-R-I-C-S and M-R-T-P-I — is common and valued. But be clear on one point: the R-T-P-I doesn't automatically recognise another body's membership. Whichever door you came through, to become chartered with the R-T-P-I you must satisfy its requirements and demonstrate its competencies.

The Experienced Practitioner route is the one most relevant to an established professional moving across. The Experienced Practitioner A-P-C is aimed at people with substantial planning experience — broadly in the range of five to ten years, depending on your qualifications — who can demonstrate the R-T-P-I competencies at a higher level. And it expressly caters for related built-environment professionals, and for those qualified overseas. It's a more demanding submission than the standard L-A-P-C, reflecting the seniority it recognises — but it doesn't require you to go back to university, if your experience is strong enough.

Or you can qualify academically. If your planning experience isn't yet deep enough for the Experienced Practitioner route, the ordinary answer applies: take an accredited — often part-time — Masters and go through the L-A-P-C, or use the degree apprenticeship if you're employed in a planning role. A surveyor with a strong development background frequently finds an accredited Masters both quick, and complementary to what they already know.

title: The degree apprenticeship route
paras: The degree apprenticeship route deserves a section of its own. The Chartered Town Planner Degree Apprenticeship — at Level 7 — has become one of the most important routes in, and it suits so many of the people this guide is written for: career-changers, planning support staff, and anyone who would rather earn than pay tuition fees.

The model is simple. You're employed in a planning role, and your employer sponsors you onto the apprenticeship. You typically work around four days a week and study one, over roughly three to four years — the exact length depends on your prior qualifications. During it, you complete an R-T-P-I accredited Masters. And at the end, you sit an end-point assessment — a professional discussion, and an A-P-C-style submission called the Degree Apprenticeship A-P-C, which is the equivalent of the ordinary A-P-C. Pass it, and you emerge with both the accredited Masters and M-R-T-P-I.

The financial point is the attraction. The training is funded through the apprenticeship levy and the employer, so the apprentice doesn't pay tuition fees. The catch is that you need an employer willing to sponsor you and to give you the study time, and you must meet the apprenticeship entry requirements. If you're already in a planning team, or can get into one, ask your employer about it. Many local authorities and consultancies actively run apprentices, because it grows their own talent.

title: The APC in detail
paras: And so to the A-P-C in detail. All roads end at the Assessment of Professional Competence. Whatever your route, this is where you prove you can practise as a chartered planner. The standard version is the Licentiate A-P-C — the L-A-P-C. The Associate, Experienced Practitioner and apprenticeship versions follow the same idea, with differences of level and format.

What do you submit? The L-A-P-C is a written submission of around five and a half thousand words — allow ten per cent either way — in three parts. First, a Practical Experience Statement, about a thousand words, setting out the range of your professional experience. Second, a Professional Competence Statement, about three thousand words — the core of the submission — in which you use one to three case studies from your own work to demonstrate all ten professional competencies the R-T-P-I assesses. And third, a Professional Development Plan, about fifteen hundred words, looking forward — identifying how you'll keep developing over the next couple of years. Confirm the current word counts and competency list on the R-T-P-I website before you write.

Alongside the statements, you provide supporting evidence. A reflective journal covering at least a year of full-time-equivalent experience. Corroborator declarations from colleagues, confirming the experience you describe. A sponsor declaration from a Chartered Town Planner who supports your application. And your degree certificates. A professional mentor isn't always compulsory, but is strongly advised — and the R-T-P-I runs a mentoring scheme to help you find one. A good mentor is the single biggest factor in a clean first-time pass.

How is it assessed — and what if you don't pass? Trained assessors read your submission against the competencies, and decide whether you've demonstrated each at the required level. If you fall short, you're referred, with feedback, and you may resubmit within two years of your first attempt without starting over. After that window, a fresh application is needed. Referral is common, and it isn't a disaster: read the feedback, fix the gaps, and resubmit. The competencies are about showing applied judgement, not reciting knowledge — so choose case studies where you made or shaped decisions, and write about what you did and why, not what your team did.

title: Time, money and CPD
paras: Let's talk about time, money and C-P-D. Time first. From an accredited Masters, expect two to three years of practice before you're chartered. From a standing start in another discipline, add the one to two years of study first. The apprenticeship is around three to four years all in, and the Associate and Experienced Practitioner routes track your experience. There's no minimum age, and no maximum time limit — people qualify in their twenties and in their fifties.

Then money. There are R-T-P-I subscription fees that vary by grade — student membership is usually free, while Licentiate and Chartered subscriptions are annual — and there's an assessment fee for the A-P-C submission. Accredited Masters courses carry tuition fees, unless you go the apprenticeship route, which is funded. Exact figures change each year, so check the current R-T-P-I fees, and the university's fees, before you plan your finances.

And C-P-D — continuing professional development — once you're chartered. Chartered status isn't the finish line. M-R-T-P-I members must undertake continuing professional development each year and keep a record of it, and must abide by the R-T-P-I Code of Professional Conduct. Get into the habit of logging your C-P-D from the start. It's a professional obligation, not an optional extra.

title: Practical tips
paras: I'll finish with some practical tips. First: check accreditation before you enrol. Only an R-T-P-I accredited course counts — and check whether it's combined or partial, so you know if a top-up is needed.
Second: start the reflective journal on day one. Reconstructing two years of experience from memory, the month before you submit, is miserable — and it shows.
Third: get a mentor early — ideally a Chartered Town Planner who will also sponsor you — and use the R-T-P-I mentoring scheme if you don't have one to hand.
Fourth: choose case studies where you had a real role. The A-P-C rewards your applied judgement, so pick work where you made decisions — not where you watched others make them.
Fifth: don't neglect the development plan section. The Professional Development Plan is part of the assessment, not a formality, and a thin one costs marks.
Sixth: if you're employed, ask about the apprenticeship. Funded training, plus a salary, plus chartered status at the end — that's hard to beat.
And seventh: talk to your local R-T-P-I network. Regional branches, Young Planners groups and A-P-C workshops are free, friendly, and full of people who've passed the thing you're about to attempt.

This guide is current to July 2026. The R-T-P-I reviews its routes, competencies, word counts, experience requirements and fees from time to time, so confirm the current detail at rtpi dot org dot uk before you commit time or money. This guide explains the routes — it isn't a substitute for the R-T-P-I's own candidate guidance.
